common_name: Lemonade Berry (Lemonade Sumac, California Lemonade Berry)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall for best establishment; lemonade berry is a California coastal native requiring excellent drainage, minimal summer water, and full sun; once established it is extremely drought tolerant and fire resistant. Best in zones 9–11 in the western US.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, lean soil in fall or early spring; lemonade berry is a coastal chaparral shrub of southern California prized for edible red-orange berries (technically drupes) covered in a sticky, tart coating — the berries can be soaked in cold water for 15–30 minutes to make a refreshing, pink, lemonade-like drink that indigenous Chumash and Cahuilla peoples have used for centuries. The berry flavor is strongly acidic and lemony with a resinous undertone. Absolutely drought-tolerant once established, thriving on coastal rainfall with no supplemental water after the first year — an ideal California-friendly landscape plant combining edibility with low maintenance and excellent wildlife value. Makes an attractive hedge or screen and tolerates salt spray.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring; three-leaf sumac adapts to a very wide range of soils and climates across the western US and is one of the most versatile native shrubs for dry gardens, slopes, and erosion control. Extremely cold hardy to -20°F once established.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil from rocky to clay; three-leaf sumac (Rhus trilobata) is a wide-ranging, cold-hardy western sumac producing small, sticky, red-orange berries used identically to lemonade berry — soaked in water to make a tart, refreshing beverage — and was one of the most important wild food and medicinal plants of western Native American tribes from the Great Plains to California. The flexible young branches were traditionally used for basket weaving by many indigenous peoples. Exceptional for erosion control on dry slopes, with beautiful orange-red fall color, excellent wildlife value, and extraordinary drought tolerance. Does not sucker aggressively. The common name 'skunkbush' refers to the strong, somewhat pungent smell of the crushed foliage.
sources: Native Plant Society of New Mexico

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root specimens in spring; winged sumac spreads by root suckers and forms colonies — excellent for naturalizing large areas, slopes, and wildlife corridors but may spread more than desired in small gardens; control by mowing the perimeter.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil in spring; winged sumac (Rhus copallinum) is the most widely distributed sumac in the eastern United States, recognizable by the distinctive 'wing' of tissue along the central leaf stem, and producing brilliant, glossy clusters of small red berries that can be used to make sumac-ade (sumac lemonade) or dried and ground as a tart, tangy spice similar to Middle Eastern sumac (Rhus coriaria). The fall foliage color of winged sumac is among the most spectacular of any native shrub — blazing scarlet, orange, and crimson in October — making it a superb ornamental as well as a food plant and wildlife species. High-value wildlife shrub providing critical late-season fruit for birds (songbirds, turkeys, quail) and dense thicket cover.

common_name: Prairie Flameleaf Sumac (Texas Sumac, Limestone Sumac)

planting.note: Plant in spring in alkaline, rocky, well-drained soil; prairie flameleaf sumac is native to Texas Hill Country limestone soils and thrives in the alkaline conditions of the Edwards Plateau and Gulf Coast — one of the best native fruiting shrubs for Texas and the Gulf Coast region.

directions: Plant in full sun in alkaline, rocky, well-drained limestone or caliche soil; prairie flameleaf sumac (Rhus lanceolata) is the primary sumac of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau, a large shrub to small tree with narrow, willow-like leaflets that turn brilliant flame-red in fall — one of the best fall color plants native to Texas and the Gulf Coast. The small, densely clustered red berries can be used to make a tart sumac lemonade or dried and ground as a spice, and are a critical winter food source for birds across the Gulf Coast. Exceptionally heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant, and adapted to the shallow, alkaline limestone soils of central and south Texas where other plants struggle. Provides dense thicket habitat for wildlife and butterfly larval host for several species.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or young container specimens in spring; Arizona walnut has a deep taproot and should be planted small; it is resistant to juglone toxicity concerns of eastern black walnut, producing juglone but in smaller quantities than Juglans nigra.

directions: Plant young trees in full sun in deep, well-drained, rocky to sandy soil in spring; Arizona walnut (Juglans major) is a native walnut of the Desert Southwest, growing along canyon bottoms and mountain slopes in Arizona, New Mexico, Texas, and northern Mexico. The small, round, thick-shelled nuts are labor-intensive to crack but have excellent, rich flavor comparable to eastern black walnut — and have been a valued food of indigenous peoples of the Southwest (Apache, Navajo, Pueblo peoples) for centuries. More heat and drought tolerant than eastern walnuts, the tree is the dominant native walnut of the semi-arid West and an outstanding choice for dry-climate food-forest systems. It is also an important wildlife tree and rootstock for grafting cultivated walnuts in desert climates.
